topic: Gas Laws & Thermodynamics — Boyle's, Charles', Ideal Gas & Laws of Thermodynamics

questions.text: Boyle's Law states that at constant temperature, for a fixed mass of gas:
options: Pressure is inversely proportional to volume
questions.explanation: Boyle's Law (1662): P ∝ 1/V at constant T. So PV = constant → P₁V₁ = P₂V₂. Doubling pressure halves volume; halving pressure doubles volume. This is why bicycle pumps heat up when you compress air, and why deep-sea bubbles grow as divers ascend.

questions.text: The second law of thermodynamics says that heat flows spontaneously:
options: From cold body to hot body
From hot body to cold body
questions.explanation: The Second Law (Clausius statement): heat never flows spontaneously from a cold body to a hot body. It always flows from higher temperature to lower temperature. A refrigerator CAN move heat from cold to hot, but only by doing work (using electrical energy) — the process is not spontaneous.

questions.text: The Universal Gas Constant R in PV = nRT has the value:
questions.explanation: R = 8.314 J/(mol·K) = 8.314 Pa·m³/(mol·K). It appears in the ideal gas law PV = nRT. The Boltzmann constant k = R/N_A = 1.38 × 10⁻²³ J/K relates to individual molecules. N_A = 6.022 × 10²³ is Avogadro's number (not a rate or energy unit).

questions.text: At STP (0°C and 1 atm), 1 mole of an ideal gas occupies:
questions.explanation: By Avogadro's Law, at STP (0°C = 273 K, 1 atm), 1 mole of ANY ideal gas occupies exactly 22.4 litres (22,400 mL). This is the molar volume of an ideal gas at STP. Whether it's H₂, O₂, N₂, or CO₂ — 1 mole occupies 22.4 L at STP.

questions.text: A gas is compressed rapidly (adiabatically). Its temperature:
options: Decreases because compression does negative work
Increases because work is done ON the gas
Depends only on the type of gas
questions.explanation: In adiabatic compression, Q = 0 (no heat exchange with surroundings). First Law: Q = ΔU + W → 0 = ΔU + W → ΔU = −W. Work is done ON the gas (W is negative for compression done on gas) → ΔU is positive → internal energy increases → temperature increases. This is why bicycle pump gets warm and diesel engines ignite fuel through compression alone.

questions.text: Charles' Law states V ∝ T. A gas at 27°C is heated until its volume doubles. Its new temperature is:
questions.explanation: Charles' Law: V₁/T₁ = V₂/T₂ (T in Kelvin!). T₁ = 27 + 273 = 300 K. V₂ = 2V₁ → T₂ = 2T₁ = 600 K = 600 − 273 = 327°C. Common trap: if you use Celsius (27 to 54°C = double in Celsius), you get the wrong answer. Always convert to Kelvin first.

questions.text: Which statement about entropy is CORRECT?
options: Entropy decreases in all natural processes
Entropy of the universe remains constant in all processes
Entropy of the universe increases in all irreversible (natural) processes
Entropy is a measure of temperature
questions.explanation: Second Law (entropy statement): In any irreversible process, total entropy of the universe (system + surroundings) increases. Entropy can decrease locally (e.g., freezing water, organising a room) but only if the surroundings' entropy increases by at least as much. For reversible processes, total entropy change = 0. Entropy never measures temperature — temperature measures average kinetic energy.

questions.text: The Carnot efficiency of a heat engine is 1 − T_C/T_H. For an engine with hot source at 500 K and cold sink at 300 K, maximum efficiency is:
questions.explanation: η_Carnot = 1 − T_C/T_H = 1 − 300/500 = 1 − 0.6 = 0.4 = 40%. This is the MAXIMUM possible efficiency for any engine operating between these temperatures. Real engines achieve less due to friction and irreversibilities. To get 100% efficiency, you'd need T_C = 0 K (absolute zero — impossible).

questions.text: Dalton's Law of Partial Pressures states that in a gas mixture:
options: All gases reach the same partial pressure
The total pressure equals the sum of individual partial pressures of each gas
Partial pressure depends on the mass of each gas
Gases with higher molecular weight have higher partial pressure
questions.explanation: Dalton's Law: P_total = P₁ + P₂ + P₃ + ... Each gas exerts pressure independently as if alone in the container. Atmospheric air at 1 atm: P(N₂) ≈ 0.78 atm + P(O₂) ≈ 0.21 atm + P(Ar) ≈ 0.009 atm + others = 1 atm. This is crucial for scuba diving — divers must avoid oxygen toxicity (high P_O₂ at depth).

questions.text: Why does a refrigerator have the condenser coils at the back (hot)?
options: To prevent the refrigerator from tipping over (counterweight)
The refrigerant releases heat absorbed from inside the fridge to the kitchen air at the back
To cool the motor that drives the compressor
The coils collect dust which acts as insulation
questions.explanation: A refrigerator is a heat engine in reverse. The refrigerant absorbs heat from inside (cold compartment) by evaporating, then is compressed and condenses at the hot condenser coils at the back — releasing that heat to the kitchen. The net effect: heat is pumped from cold (inside) to hot (kitchen), requiring electrical work input (Second Law: this doesn't happen spontaneously).

questions.text: Which law of thermodynamics is the basis for the operation of thermometers?
questions.explanation: The Zeroth Law: if A is in thermal equilibrium with C, and B is in thermal equilibrium with C, then A and B are in thermal equilibrium. A thermometer (C) equilibrates with the body being measured (A) and displays a reading. By the Zeroth Law, the thermometer reading accurately represents the body's temperature, and two bodies at the same thermometer reading are at the same temperature.

questions.text: Newton's calculation of the speed of sound in air gave an incorrect (lower) value because he assumed sound propagation is isothermal. Laplace corrected it by using:
options: Adiabatic (no heat exchange) propagation giving a higher speed
He used the real gas equation instead of ideal gas law
questions.explanation: Sound waves compress and rarefy air too rapidly for heat exchange to occur — the process is adiabatic, not isothermal. For adiabatic processes, the effective 'bulk modulus' is γ times larger (γ = Cp/Cv ≈ 1.4 for air). Speed of sound = √(γP/ρ) instead of √(P/ρ). Laplace's correction gives v ≈ 332 m/s at 0°C, matching experiment, vs Newton's ~279 m/s.

questions.text: Gay-Lussac's Law relates pressure and temperature at constant volume. A sealed rigid container is heated from 300 K to 600 K. Its pressure:
questions.explanation: Gay-Lussac's Law: P/T = constant (at constant V). P₂/P₁ = T₂/T₁ = 600/300 = 2. Pressure doubles when absolute temperature doubles. Example: pressure cooker sealed and heated — both P and T increase proportionally. This is also why tyre pressures should be checked cold (before driving heats them).

questions.text: The first law of thermodynamics is a statement of:
questions.explanation: The First Law of Thermodynamics is the law of conservation of energy applied to thermodynamic systems: Q = ΔU + W (heat added = change in internal energy + work done by system). Energy cannot be created or destroyed. It rules out 'perpetual motion machines of the first kind' (machines that create energy from nothing).

questions.text: Which of the following is correct about an ideal gas?
options: Ideal gas molecules have significant volume and intermolecular forces
Ideal gas obeys PV = nRT at all temperatures and pressures
Real gases behave ideally at high pressure and low temperature
Ideal gases condense to liquid at low temperatures
questions.explanation: An ideal gas perfectly obeys PV = nRT under all conditions. Ideal gas assumptions: molecules have no volume; no intermolecular forces; elastic collisions only. Real gases deviate from ideal behaviour at HIGH pressure (volume of molecules matters) and LOW temperature (intermolecular forces matter, and gases can condense). Real gases approach ideal behaviour at LOW pressure and HIGH temperature.

questions.text: The phenomenon that makes a hot air balloon rise is based on:
options: Boyle's Law — heated air at constant volume has higher pressure
Charles' Law — heated air at constant pressure expands and becomes less dense
Gay-Lussac's Law — heated sealed air has higher pressure
Dalton's Law — different gases in the balloon have different partial pressures
questions.explanation: Hot air balloon: the balloon is open at the bottom (constant atmospheric pressure). When air inside is heated, it expands (Charles' Law: V ∝ T at constant P). The expanded, hot air is less dense than surrounding cool air. When the balloon + envelope weight < weight of displaced air → net buoyant force upward → balloon rises (Archimedes + Charles' Law combined).
